title: Types and methods should avoid having too many generic parameters
description: Types and methods should not have too many generic parametersAnalyze your codeadaptability-focusedmaintainabilityCode Smellbrain-overloadWhy is this an issue?A method or class with too many type parameters has likely aggregated too many responsibilities and should be split.Noncompliant code exampleWith the default parameter value of 2:<S, T, U, V> void foo() {} // Noncompliant; not really readable <String, Integer, Object, String>foo(); // especially on invocationsAvailable In:Catch issues on the fly,in your IDEDetect issues in your GitHub, Azure DevOps Services, Bitbucket Cloud, GitLab repositoriesAnalyze code in youron-premise CIAvailable Since9.1Analyze code in youron-premise CIDeveloper EditionAvailable Since9.1©2025SonarSource Sàrl. All rights reserved.Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Ter
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

logic.detection_conditions: Count generic type parameters in class, interface, struct, and method declarations. Flag when count exceeds threshold (default: 2)

conditions: Type or method declaration with generic parameters <T1, T2, ...>
Number of generic parameters exceeds threshold (default: 2)
Not a comment or string literal

never_trigger_on: Types with 2 or fewer generic parameters
logic.reasoning: Types and methods with too many generic parameters are hard to read, understand, and use. They often indicate that the type/method has aggregated too many responsibilities and should be split into smaller, more focused components.

should_trigger: public class DataProcessor<T1, T2, T3, T4> { }
public void Map<TSource, TIntermediate, TDestination, TResult>() { }
public interface IProcessor<TIn, TOut, TConfig, TResult> { }
should_not_trigger: // Class<T1, T2, T3> would be bad
